ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code specific to Oreo-type sandwich biscuit; A0EJX (sweet biscuits and cookies) used as the closest defensible parent match.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for commercial sandwich biscuit product.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Oreo is approximately 3 biscuits (34 g), which is the portion size commonly listed on the product packaging.

nutrient_source.title: Oreo Original Chocolate Sandwich Cookies — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record was sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95. All per_100g values are taken directly from the HPB record. Saturated fat not reported by HPB; estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for Oreo cookies (USDA FDC ID 167512) at 1.7 g/100g and documented accordingly.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Foster-Powell K, Holt SHA, Brand-Miller JC. American Journal of Clinical Nutrition 76(1):5–56.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 71 is based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for sweet plain biscuits/cookies (wheat flour, sugar base), which is the closest approved analogous food category for Oreo-type sandwich biscuits. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × available carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100 = 71 × 8.7 / 100 ≈ 6.2 (serving size 34 g, carbohydrate 25.6 g/100g → 8.7 g available carbohydrate per serving; total carbohydrate per serving = 25.6 × 0.34 = 8.7 g).

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (21.2 g/100g) with negligible dietary fibre (0.1 g/100g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and promotes weight gain, both of which elevate blood pressure.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Oreo biscuits due to their high sugar load (21.2 g/100g) and negligible fibre, which promote insulin-driven sodium retention and weight gain that raise blood pressure, despite the relatively low sodium content of 75 mg/100g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 21.2 g/100g exceeds the 20 g/100g glycaemic ceiling; combined with negligible dietary fibre (0.1 g/100g) and refined wheat flour, Oreo biscuits cause rapid postprandial blood glucose spikes.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid Oreo biscuits or limit intake to no more than 1–2 biscuits on rare occasions, as the high sugar content (21.2 g/100g), high GI (~71), and near-zero fibre (0.1 g/100g) combine to produce rapid and pronounced blood glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Ultra-processed formulation with palm oil (saturated fat ~1.7 g/100g), high sugar (21.2 g/100g), and refined flour contributes to dyslipidaemia, visceral adiposity, and systemic inflammation relevant to cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should minimise consumption of Oreo biscuits, as their ultra-processed composition — including palm oil-derived saturated fat, high added sugar, and refined carbohydrates — promotes LDL elevation, triglyceride accumulation, and chronic low-grade inflammation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sugar at 21.2 g/100g exceeds the 15 g RA threshold; palm oil contributes pro-inflammatory saturated fat; alkali-processed cocoa provides no meaningful anti-inflammatory polyphenol benefit.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid Oreo biscuits due to their high sugar content (21.2 g/100g) and palm oil-derived saturated fat, both of which promote systemic inflammation and may exacerbate RA disease activity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Oreo is a globally recognised ultra-processed chocolate sandwich biscuit manufactured by Nabisco (Mondelez International), consisting of two round cocoa-flavoured wafers enclosing a sweet vanilla cream filling. The wafers are made from enriched wheat flour, alkali-processed cocoa powder, sugar, and palm oil, baked to a crisp texture with a mildly bitter chocolate flavour balanced by the intensely sweet cream centre. Oreo biscuits are sold worldwide and are widely available in Singapore supermarkets and convenience stores. Per 100 g, Oreo provides approximately 165 kcal, 3.97 g protein, 5.66 g fat, 25.6 g carbohydrate, and 21.2 g sugar, with negligible dietary fibre (0.1 g) and moderate sodium (75 mg). The high sugar content and near-zero fibre make Oreo clinically unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or rheumatoid arthritis. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 71 (high GI), reflecting rapid glucose absorption driven by refined flour and high sugar. Oreo is classified as ultra-processed (NOVA Group 4) and contains wheat gluten and soy allergens. It is vegetarian but not vegan due to potential dairy cross-contact. The product is widely consumed as a snack across all age groups in Singapore and Southeast Asia.
common_user_queries: How much sugar is in Oreo biscuits?
Can I eat Oreo if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of Oreo cookies?
How many calories in 3 Oreo biscuits?

confidence_reason: Core nutritional values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 167512) as the closest approved secondary source. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous food category (sweet plain biscuits). All mandatory fields are populated from approved sources.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient vitamins, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo record for Oreo, and granular breakdown not available in the HPB database as of 2026. saturated_fat_g estimated at 1.7 g/100g from USDA FoodData Central entry for Oreo cookies (FDC ID 167512) as HPB does not report this field; this is an approved secondary source adaptation. Input record incorrectly classified Oreo under food_group 'Grains and staples' and food_subgroup 'Baking ingredients' and assigned primary_cuisine 'Indian' with occasion_tags 'lunch, dinner'; these have been corrected to food_group 'Snacks', food_subgroup 'Sweet biscuits and cookies', cuisine 'Global / Commercial', and region_of_origin 'United States'. GI of 71 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for sweet plain biscuits/cookies; no direct Oreo GI entry exists in the approved GI database. GL per serving calculated as 71 × (25.6 × 0.34) / 100 = 71 × 8.7 / 100 ≈ 6.2 for a 34 g (3-biscuit) serving. All core macros (protein_g 3.97, fat_g 5.66, carbohydrate_g 25.6) are non-zero and sourced from HPB; no zero-macro justification required. foodex2_match_type corrected from 'broader' to 'closest_parent' to conform to the allowed enumeration values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'].

Corrected cuisine from 'Indian' to 'Global / Commercial' and region_of_origin to 'United States'.
